reasoning_content: We need to check if 263 is prime. Let's test divisibility by small primes: 2: odd, not divisible. 3: sum of digits 2+6+3=11, not divisible by 3. 5: doesn't end in 0 or 5. 7: 7*37=259, 7*38=266, so 263-259=4, not divisible. 11: 11*23=253, 11*24=264, so no. 13: 13*20=260, 13*21=273, so 263-260=3, not. 17: 17*15=255, 17*16=272, difference 8, no. 19: 19*13=247, 19*14=266, 263-247=16, no. 23: 23*11=253, 23*12=276, 263-253=10, no. 29: 29*9=261, 29*10=290, 263-261=2, no. 31: 31*8=248, 31*9=279, 263-248=15, no. 37: 37*7=259, 37*8=296, 263-259=4, no. 41: 41*6=246, 41*7=287, 263-246=17, no. 43: 43*6=258, 43*7=301, 263-258=5, no. 47: 47*5=235, 47*6=282, 263-235=28, no. 53: 53*4=212, 53*5=265, too high, 263-212=51, not divisible. Next prime sqrt(263) ≈ 16.2, actually sqrt(263) ≈ 16.2? Wait 16^2=256, 17^2=289, so we need to test primes up to 16, but we already tested 2,3,5,7,11,13. That's all primes ≤16. So 263 is prime. But let's double-check: 17 is greater than sqrt? Actually sqrt(263)=16.2, so primes up to 13 are enough. We tested 2,3,5,7,11,13. None divide. So 263 is prime. Quick sanity check: yes, prime.
